subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write chemical equations for the following reactions: Reaction of ethanolic NH 3 with C 2 H 5 Cl. Ammonolysis of benzyl chloride and reaction of amine so formed with two moles of CH 3 Cl.
question_html:
Write chemical equations for the following reactions:

  1. Reaction of ethanolic NH3 with C2H5Cl.
  2. Ammonolysis of benzyl chloride and reaction of amine so formed with two moles of CH3Cl.
solution_text: Chemical equation in the following reaction is: The reaction of ethanolic NH 3 with C 2 H 5 Cl. Ammonolysis of benzyl chloride and the reaction of amine so formed with 2 moles of CH 3 Cl.
solution_html:
Chemical equation in the following reaction is:

  1. The reaction of ethanolic NH3 with C2H5Cl.
  2. Ammonolysis of benzyl chloride and the reaction of amine so formed with 2 moles of CH3Cl.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write structure and IUPAC names of: The amide which gives propanamide by Hoffmann bromamide reaction. The amine produced by the Hoffmann degradation of benzamide.
question_html:
Write structure and IUPAC names of:

  1. The amide which gives propanamide by Hoffmann bromamide reaction. 
  2. The amine produced by the Hoffmann degradation of benzamide.
solution_text: Propanamine contains three carbons. Hence, the amide molecule must contain four carbon atoms. Structure and IUPAC name of the starting amide with four carbon atoms is as follows: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {{\text{ }}C}\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - N{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{Butanamide}}} {/tex} Benzamide is an aromatic amide containing seven carbon atoms. Hence, the amine formed from benzamide is aromatic primary amine containing six carbon atoms.
solution_html:
1. Propanamine contains three carbons. Hence, the amide molecule must contain four carbon atoms. Structure and IUPAC name of the starting amide with four carbon atoms is as follows:
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2} - C{H_2} - \mathop {{\text{ }}C}\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - N{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{Butanamide}}} {/tex}
  2. Benzamide is an aromatic amide containing seven carbon atoms. Hence, the amine formed from benzamide is aromatic primary amine containing six carbon atoms.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Arrange the following in decreasing order of their basic strength: C 6 H 5 NH 2 , C 2 H 5 NH 2 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, NH 3
question_html: Arrange the following in decreasing order of their basic strength: C6H5NH2, C2H5NH2, (C2H5)2 NH, NH3
solution_text: The decreasing order of basic strength of the above amines and ammonia (NH 3 ) follows in the following order: (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH > C 2 H 5 NH 2 > NH 3 > C 6 H 5 NH 2
solution_html:
The decreasing order of basic strength of the above amines and ammonia (NH3) follows in the following order:
(C2H5)2 NH > C2H5NH2 > NH3 > C6H5NH2

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How will you convert 4-nitrotoluene to 2-bromobenzoic acid?
question_html: How will you convert 4-nitrotoluene to 2-bromobenzoic acid?
solution_text: Following reaction involved in the conversion of 4-nitrotoluene to 2- Bromo benzoic acid: Nitro compounds are reduced to an amine by Sn/HCl. Aliphatic amine reacts with nitrous acid to form an aliphatic diazonium salt. Mild reducing agents like hypophosphorous acid to reduce diazonium salts.
solution_html:
Following reaction involved in the conversion of 4-nitrotoluene to 2- Bromo benzoic acid:

  1. Nitro compounds are reduced to an amine by Sn/HCl.
  2. Aliphatic amine reacts with nitrous acid to form an aliphatic diazonium salt.
  3. Mild reducing agents like hypophosphorous acid to reduce diazonium salts.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Classify the following amines as primary, secondary or tertiary: (C 2 H 5 ) 2 CHNH 2 (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH
question_html:
Classify the following amines as primary, secondary or tertiary:

  3. (C2H5)2CHNH2
  4. (C2H5)2NH
solution_text: Primary amine: (i) and (iii) Secondary amine: (iv) Tertiary amine: (ii)
solution_html:
Primary amine: (i) and (iii)
Secondary amine: (iv)
Tertiary amine: (ii)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write structures of different isomeric amines corresponding to the molecular formula, C 4 H 11 N. Write IUPAC names of all the isomers. What type of isomerism is exhibited by different pairs of amines?
question_html:
1. Write structures of different isomeric amines corresponding to the molecular formula, C4H11N.
  2. Write IUPAC names of all the isomers.
  3. What type of isomerism is exhibited by different pairs of amines?
solution_text: (i), (ii) The structures and IUPAC names of different isomeric amines corresponding to the molecular formula, {tex}{{\text{C}}_{\text{4}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{11}}}}{\text{N}}{/tex} are given below: {tex}CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-NH_2 {/tex} Butanamine {tex}\left( {1^\circ } \right){/tex} {tex}CH_3-CH_2-{\overset {NH_2}{\overset{|} CH}}-CH_3 {/tex} Butan-2-amine {tex}\left( {1^\circ } \right){/tex} {tex}CH_3-{\overset {CH_3}{\overset{|} CH}}-CH_2-NH_2{/tex} 2-Methylpropanamine {tex}\left( {1^\circ } \right){/tex} {tex}CH_3-{\overset {CH_3}{\overset{|}{\underset{CH_3} {\underset{|} C}}}}-NH_2{/tex} 2-Methylpropan-2-amine (1 o ) {tex}CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-NH-CH_3 {/tex} N-Methylpropanamine {tex}\left( {2^\circ } \right){/tex} {tex}CH_3-CH_2-NH-CH_2-CH_3{/tex} N-Ethylethanamine {tex}\left( {2^\circ } \right){/tex} {tex}CH_3-{\overset {CH_3}{\overset{|} CH}}-NH-CH_3{/tex} N-Methylpropan-2-amine {tex}\left( {2^\circ } \right){/tex} {tex}CH_3-CH_2-{\overset {CH_3}{\overset{|} N}}-CH_3{/tex} N, N- Dimethylethanamine {tex}\left( {3^\circ } \right){/tex} (iii) The pairs (a) and (b) and (e) and (g) exhibit position isomerism. The pairs (a) and (c); (a) and (d); (b) and (c); (b) and (d) exhibit chain isomerism. The pairs (e) and (f) and (f) and (g) exhibit metamerism. All primary amines exhibit functional isomerism with secondary and tertiary amines and vice-versa.
solution_html:
(i), (ii) The structures and IUPAC names of different isomeric amines corresponding to the molecular formula, {tex}{{\text{C}}_{\text{4}}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{11}}}}{\text{N}}{/tex} are given below:

  1. {tex}CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-NH_2 {/tex}
    Butanamine{tex}\left( {1^\circ } \right){/tex}
  2. {tex}CH_3-CH_2-{\overset {NH_2}{\overset{|} CH}}-CH_3 {/tex}
    Butan-2-amine {tex}\left( {1^\circ } \right){/tex}
  3. {tex}CH_3-{\overset {CH_3}{\overset{|} CH}}-CH_2-NH_2{/tex}
    2-Methylpropanamine {tex}\left( {1^\circ } \right){/tex}
  4. {tex}CH_3-{\overset {CH_3}{\overset{|}{\underset{CH_3} {\underset{|} C}}}}-NH_2{/tex}
    2-Methylpropan-2-amine (1o)
  5. {tex}CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-NH-CH_3 {/tex}
    N-Methylpropanamine {tex}\left( {2^\circ } \right){/tex}
  6. {tex}CH_3-CH_2-NH-CH_2-CH_3{/tex}
    N-Ethylethanamine {tex}\left( {2^\circ } \right){/tex}
  7. {tex}CH_3-{\overset {CH_3}{\overset{|} CH}}-NH-CH_3{/tex}
    N-Methylpropan-2-amine {tex}\left( {2^\circ } \right){/tex} 
  8. {tex}CH_3-CH_2-{\overset {CH_3}{\overset{|} N}}-CH_3{/tex}
    N, N- Dimethylethanamine {tex}\left( {3^\circ } \right){/tex}

(iii) The pairs (a) and (b) and (e) and (g) exhibit position isomerism.
The pairs (a) and (c); (a) and (d); (b) and (c); (b) and (d) exhibit chain isomerism.
The pairs (e) and (f) and (f) and (g) exhibit metamerism.
All primary amines exhibit functional isomerism with secondary and tertiary amines and vice-versa.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Arrange the following in increasing order of their basic strength: C 2 H 5 NH 2 , C 6 H 5 NH 2 , NH 3 , C 6 H 5 CH 2 NH 2 and (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH C 2 H 5 NH 2, (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N and C 6 H 5 NH 2 CH 3 NH 2, (CH 3 ) 2 NH, (CH 3 ) 3 N, C 6 H 5 NH 2 , C 6 H 5 CH 2 NH 2
question_html:
Arrange the following in increasing order of their basic strength:

  1. C2H5NH2, C6H5NH2, NH3, C6H5 CH2NH2 and (C2H5)2NH
  2. C2H5NH2, (C2H5)2NH, (C2H5)3N and C6H5NH2
  3. CH3NH2, (CH3)2NH, (CH3)3N, C6H5NH2, C6H5CH2NH2
solution_text: C 6 H 5 NH 2 <NH 3 <C 6 H 5 CH 2 NH 2 <C 2 H 5 NH 2 <(C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH C 6 H 5 NH 2 <C 2 H 5 NH 2 <(C2H 5 ) 3 N<(C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH C 6 H 5 NH 2 <C 6 H 5 CH 2 NH 2 <(CH 3 ) 3 N<CH 3 NH 2 <(CH 3 ) 2 NH
solution_html:
1. C6H5NH2<NH3<C6H5CH2NH2<C2H5NH2<(C2H5)2NH
  2. C6H5NH2<C2H5NH2<(C2H5)3N<(C2H5)2NH
  3. C6H5NH2<C6H5CH2NH2<(CH3)3N<CH3NH2<(CH3)2NH

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Complete the following acid-base reactions and name the products: CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 NH 2 + HCl {tex}\to{/tex} (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N + HCl {tex}\to{/tex}
question_html:
Complete the following acid-base reactions and name the products:

  1.  CH3CH2CH2NH2 + HCl {tex}\to{/tex}
  2. (C2H5)3N + HCl {tex}\to{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}N{H_2}\;}\limits_{n - \Pr opyla\min e} + \;HCl \to \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}\mathop {N{H_3}}\limits^ + \mathop {Cl}\limits^ - }\limits_{n - \Pr opylammonium\;chloride} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {{{({C_2}{H_5})}_3}N\;}\limits_{Triethyla\min e} + \;HCl \to \mathop {{{({C_2}{H_5})}_3}\mathop {NH}\limits^ + \mathop {Cl}\limits^ - }\limits_{Triethylammonium\;chloride} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}N{H_2}\;}\limits_{n - \Pr opyla\min e} + \;HCl \to \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}\mathop {N{H_3}}\limits^ + \mathop {Cl}\limits^ - }\limits_{n - \Pr opylammonium\;chloride} {/tex}
     
  2. {tex}\mathop {{{({C_2}{H_5})}_3}N\;}\limits_{Triethyla\min e} + \;HCl \to \mathop {{{({C_2}{H_5})}_3}\mathop {NH}\limits^ + \mathop {Cl}\limits^ - }\limits_{Triethylammonium\;chloride} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the reaction of the final alkylation product of aniline with excess of methyl iodide in the presence of sodium carbonate solution.
question_html: Write the reaction of the final alkylation product of aniline with excess of methyl iodide in the presence of sodium carbonate solution.
solution_text: Aniline reacts with methyl iodide to produce N, N-dimethylaniline. With excess methyl iodide, in the presence of Na 2 CO 3 solution, N, N-dimethylaniline produces N, N, N-trimethylanilinium carbonate.
solution_html:
Aniline reacts with methyl iodide to produce N, N-dimethylaniline.

With excess methyl iodide, in the presence of Na2CO3 solution, N, N-dimethylaniline produces N, N, N-trimethylanilinium carbonate.

question_text: Write chemical reaction of aniline with benzoyl chloride and write the name of the product obtained.
question_html: Write chemical reaction of aniline with benzoyl chloride and write the name of the product obtained.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write structures of different isomers corresponding to the molecular formula, C 3 H 9 N. Write IUPAC names of the isomers which will liberate nitrogen gas on treatment with nitrous acid.
question_html: Write structures of different isomers corresponding to the molecular formula, C3H9N. Write IUPAC names of the isomers which will liberate nitrogen gas on treatment with nitrous acid.
solution_text: The structures of different isomers corresponding to the molecular formula C 3 H 9 N are given below: {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - N{H_2}{/tex} Propan -1-amine {tex}\left( {1^\circ } \right){/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {CH}\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{N{H_2}} } - C{H_3} {/tex} Propan-2-amine (2 o ) {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - NH - {C_2}{H_5}}{/tex} N-methylmethanamine (2 o ) {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop N\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } - C{H_3}{/tex} N, N-Dimethylmethanamine {tex}\left( {3^\circ } \right){/tex} Primary amines, (a) propan-1-amine, and (b) Propan-2-amine will liberate nitrogen gas on treatment with nitrous acid. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}N{H_2}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 1 - a\min e} \; + \;HN{O_2} \to \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{\Pr opan - 1 - ol} \; + \;{N_2}\; + \;HCl{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits_| }\limits_{N{H_2}} - C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - a\min e} \; + \;HN{O_2} \to \mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} H - C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} \; + \;{N_2}\; + \;HCl{/tex}
solution_html:
The structures of different isomers corresponding to the molecular formula C3H9N are given below:

  1.  {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - N{H_2}{/tex}
    Propan -1-amine {tex}\left( {1^\circ } \right){/tex}
  2. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {CH}\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{N{H_2}} } - C{H_3} {/tex}
    Propan-2-amine (2o)
  3. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - NH - {C_2}{H_5}}{/tex} 
    N-methylmethanamine (2o)
  4. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop N\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } - C{H_3}{/tex}
    N, N-Dimethylmethanamine {tex}\left( {3^\circ } \right){/tex}

Primary amines, (a) propan-1-amine, and (b) Propan-2-amine will liberate nitrogen gas on treatment with nitrous acid.
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}N{H_2}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 1 - a\min e} \; + \;HN{O_2} \to \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{\Pr opan - 1 - ol} \; + \;{N_2}\; + \;HCl{/tex}

{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits_| }\limits_{N{H_2}} - C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - a\min e} \; + \;HN{O_2} \to \mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} H - C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} \; + \;{N_2}\; + \;HCl{/tex}

question_text: Convert 3-Methylaniline into 3-nitrotoluene Aniline into 1, 3, 5-tribromobenzene.
question_html:
Convert

  1. 3-Methylaniline into 3-nitrotoluene
  2. Aniline into 1, 3, 5-tribromobenzene.
solution_html: 1.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write IUPAC names of the following compounds and classify them into primary, secondary, and tertiary amines. (CH 3 ) 2 CHNH 2 CH 3 (CH 2 ) 2 NH 2 CH 3 NHCH(CH 3 ) 2 (CH 3 ) 3 CNH 2 C 6 H 5 NHCH 3 CH 3 CH 2 ) 2 NCH 3 m–BrC 6 H 4 NH 2
question_html:
Write IUPAC names of the following compounds and classify them into primary, secondary, and tertiary amines.

  1. (CH3)2CHNH2
  2. CH3(CH2)2NH2
  3. CH3NHCH(CH3)2
  4. (CH3)3CNH2
  5. C6H5NHCH3
  6. CH3CH2)2NCH3
  7. m–BrC6H4NH2
solution_text: 1-Methylethanamine (primary amine) The root name is based on the longest chain with the -NH 2 attached. The chain is numbered so as to give the amine unit the lowest possible number. The longest chain is ethane chain which is further suffixed with ‘amine’ written as 1-methylethanamine. Propan-1-amine (primary amine) The longest chain here is propane containing 3 carbon atom. The naming is such that the amine unit should get the lowest possible number. Propane-1-amine can also be written as 1-propylamine. N−Methyl-2-methylethanamine (secondary amine) The chain is numbered so as to give the amine unit the lowest possible number. The other alkyl group is treated as a substituent, with N as the locant. The N locant is listed before numerical locants. The longest chain is ethane which has a Methyl substituent group. 2-Methylpropan-2-amine (primary amine) Propane is the longest chain and is joined to the amine group at the 2 nd position. Another substituent methyl group is also in the same position. N−Methylbenzamine or N-methylaniline (secondary amine) Aniline is considered as the principle group. The other alkyl group is treated as a substituent, with N as the locant. N-Ethyl-N-methylethanamine(tertiary amine) This is a tertiary group of amine. The root name is based on the longest chain with an amine having the lowest possible number. The alkyl groups are treated as substituents with N as the locant. 3-Bromobenzenamine or 3-bromoaniline (primary amine) Benzene is considered the principle group. The substituent is bromine which is numbered as 3.Conventional method of naming is followed as.
solution_html:
1. 1-Methylethanamine (primary amine)
    The root name is based on the longest chain with the -NH2 attached. The chain is numbered so as to give the amine unit the lowest possible number. The longest chain is ethane chain which is further suffixed with ‘amine’ written as 1-methylethanamine.

  2. Propan-1-amine (primary amine)
    The longest chain here is propane containing 3 carbon atom. The naming is such that the amine unit should get the lowest possible number. Propane-1-amine can also be written as 1-propylamine.

  3. N−Methyl-2-methylethanamine (secondary amine)
    The chain is numbered so as to give the amine unit the lowest possible number. The other alkyl group is treated as a substituent, with N as the locant. The N locant is listed before numerical locants. The longest chain is ethane which has a Methyl substituent group.

  4. 2-Methylpropan-2-amine (primary amine)
    Propane is the longest chain and is joined to the amine group at the 2nd position. Another substituent methyl group is also in the same position.

  5. N−Methylbenzamine or N-methylaniline (secondary amine)
    Aniline is considered as the principle group. The other alkyl group is treated as a substituent, with N as the locant.

  6. N-Ethyl-N-methylethanamine(tertiary amine)
    This is a tertiary group of amine. The root name is based on the longest chain with an amine having the lowest possible number. The alkyl groups are treated as substituents with N as the locant.

  7. 3-Bromobenzenamine or 3-bromoaniline (primary amine)
    Benzene is considered the principle group. The substituent is bromine which is numbered as 3.Conventional method of naming is followed as.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Give one chemical test to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds: Methylamine and dimethylamine Secondary and tertiary amines Ethylamine and aniline Aniline and benzylamine Aniline and N-methyl aniline
question_html:
Give one chemical test to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds:

  1. Methylamine and dimethylamine 
  2. Secondary and tertiary amines 
  3. Ethylamine and aniline 
  4. Aniline and benzylamine 
  5. Aniline and N-methyl aniline
solution_text: These can be distinguished by the carbylamine test. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}N{H_2} + }\limits_{\mathop {Methyla\min e}\limits_{({1^0}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} A\min e)} } CHC{l_3} + \mathop {3KOH}\limits_{(alc)} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {C{H_3}NC + 3KCl + 3{H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Methylisocyanide} \\ {(offensive{\kern 1pt} smell)} \end{array}} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}NH}\limits_{\mathop {Dimethyla\min e}\limits_{({2^0})\,a\min e)} } \,\xrightarrow{{CHC{l_3}/KOH(alc)}}No\,reaction{/tex} ​ Secondary and tertiary amines can be distinguished by Libermann nitrosoamine test. 2° amines react with nitrous acid to form N-nitrosamines while 3° do not. {tex}\mathop {{{(C{H_3}C{H_2})}_2}}\limits_{Diethyla\min e} NH + HO - N = 0 \to \mathop {(C{H_3}CH_2)_2N - N = O + {H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {N - Nitrosodiethyla\min e} \\ {(Yellow\,\,colour)} \end{array}} {/tex} Azo dye test: Aniline and ethylamine can be distinguished by azo dye test. It involves the reaction of aniline with HNO 2 at 273-278 K followed by treatment with an alkaline solution of β-naphthol, which gives a brilliant yellow, orange or red coloured dye. Ethylamine under these condition gives a brisk evolution of N 2 gas with the formation of primary alcohol. Nitrous acid test: Benzylamine reacts with nitrous acid to form a diazonium salt which being unstable even at low temperature, decomposes with evolution of N 2 gas. Aniline, on the other hand, reacts with nitrous acid to form benzene diazonium chloride which is stable at 273 - 278 K and hence does not decompose to evolve N 2 gas. Carbylamine test: Aniline being a primary amine gives carbylamine test whereas N-methylamine being a secondary amine does not give this test. when aniline is heated with an alcoholic solution of KOH and CHCl 3, it gives the offensive smell of phenyl isocyanide.
solution_html:
1. These can be distinguished by the carbylamine test.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}N{H_2} + }\limits_{\mathop {Methyla\min e}\limits_{({1^0}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} A\min e)} } CHC{l_3} + \mathop {3KOH}\limits_{(alc)} \xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {C{H_3}NC + 3KCl + 3{H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Methylisocyanide} \\ {(offensive{\kern 1pt} smell)} \end{array}} {/tex}
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}NH}\limits_{\mathop {Dimethyla\min e}\limits_{({2^0})\,a\min e)} } \,\xrightarrow{{CHC{l_3}/KOH(alc)}}No\,reaction{/tex}​
  2. Secondary and tertiary amines can be distinguished by Libermann nitrosoamine test. 2° amines react with nitrous acid to form N-nitrosamines while 3° do not.
    {tex}\mathop {{{(C{H_3}C{H_2})}_2}}\limits_{Diethyla\min e} NH + HO - N = 0 \to \mathop {(C{H_3}CH_2)_2N - N = O + {H_2}O}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {N - Nitrosodiethyla\min e} \\ {(Yellow\,\,colour)} \end{array}} {/tex}
  3. Azo dye test: Aniline and ethylamine can be distinguished by azo dye test. It involves the reaction of aniline with HNO2 at 273-278 K followed by treatment with an alkaline solution of β-naphthol, which gives a brilliant yellow, orange or red coloured dye. Ethylamine under these condition gives a brisk evolution of N2 gas with the formation of primary alcohol.
  4. Nitrous acid test: Benzylamine reacts with nitrous acid to form a diazonium salt which being unstable even at low temperature, decomposes with evolution of N2 gas.
    Aniline, on the other hand, reacts with nitrous acid to form benzene diazonium chloride which is stable at 273 - 278 K and hence does not decompose to evolve N2 gas.
  5. Carbylamine test: Aniline being a primary amine gives carbylamine test whereas N-methylamine being a secondary amine does not give this test. when aniline is heated with an alcoholic solution of KOH and CHCl3, it gives the offensive smell of phenyl isocyanide.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Account for the following: pK b of aniline is more than that of methylamine. Ethylamine is soluble in water whereas aniline is not. Methylamine in water reacts with ferric chloride to precipitate hydrated ferric oxide. Although the amino group is o– and p–directing in aromatic electrophilic substitution reactions, aniline on nitration gives a substantial amount of m-nitroaniline. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is preferred for synthesizing primary amines.
question_html:
Account for the following:

  1. pKb of aniline is more than that of methylamine.
  2. Ethylamine is soluble in water whereas aniline is not.
  3. Methylamine in water reacts with ferric chloride to precipitate hydrated ferric oxide.
  4. Although the amino group is o– and p–directing in aromatic electrophilic substitution reactions, aniline on nitration gives a substantial amount of m-nitroaniline.
  5. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  6. Diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines.
  7. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is preferred for synthesizing primary amines.
solution_text: Pk b value is the negative logarithm of the basicity constant (K b ) .i.e., pK b = -logK b Evidently, smaller the value of pK b , stronger is the base (strong tendency to donate electrons) = The structure of methylamine is CH 3 NH 2 = The structure of aniline is: Aniline (C 6 H 5 NH 2 ) shows resonance: As a result of resonance, the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom gets delocalized over the benzene ring. As a result, electron density on the nitrogen decreases and thus is less easily available to donate electrons making it less basic. In contrast, in methylamine (CH 3 NH 2 ), the delocalization of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom by resonance is not possible. Furthermore, CH 3 being an electron- releasing group, due to which +I effect of CH 3 increases the electron density on the N- atom and thus is more easily available to donate electrons making it more basic. Therefore, aniline is a weaker base than methylamine and hence its pK b value is higher than that of methylamine. Ethylamine when added to water form H-bonds with water. It dissolves in water due to intermolecular H-bonding as shown below: Thus, ethylamine is soluble in water. However, in aniline, due to the larger hydrophobic part, i.e., hydrocarbon part (C 6 H 5 group), which tends to retard the formation of H-bonds. The extent of H-bonding decreases and hence aniline is insoluble in water. The structure of methylamine is CH 3 NH 2 Methylamine is more basic than water due to the presence of CH 3 (electron releasing group) and +I effect (pushing electrons). Being more basic, methylamine accepts a proton from water liberating OH - ions. Dissociation of ferric chloride in water to give Fe 3+ and Cl - FeCl 3 → Fe 3+ + 3Cl - Now, the liberated OH - ions combine with Fe 3+ ions present in H 2 O to form a brown precipitate of hydrated ferric oxide. The below diagram shows the position of ortho(o), para(p) and meta(m) derivatives of the amino group: Electrophilic addition reaction of amines: In addition to the reaction of the amino group (NH 2 group), aromatic amines also undergo typical electrophilic substitution reactions of the aromatic ring. In all these reactions, the NH 2 group strongly activates the aromatic ring through delocalization of the lone pair of electrons of the N-atom over the aromatic ring. As a result, electron density increases more at ortho and para positions. Therefore NH 2 group directs the incoming group to ortho and para positions, i.e., NH 2 is an o-, p-directing group. But it has been observed that on nitration of aniline gives a substantial amount of m-nitro aniline. Explanation: Nitration is usually carried out in acidic medium in the presence of concentrated HNO 3 and concentrated H 2 SO 4 . As a result, most of the aniline is converted into anilinium ion(gets protonated) and since - + NH 3 is an m-directing group, therefore, an unexpectedly large amount of m-nitroaniline is obtained. Nitration of aniline gives mainly p-nitroaniline Nitration of anilinium ions give m-nitro aniline(due to protonation) Hence, the nitration of aniline gives a mixture of p-nitroaniline and m-nitroaniline in approx. 1:1 ratio. Friedel- Crafts reaction: When any benzene or its derivative is treated with an alkyl halide (R-X, X=Cl) or acetyl chloride (CH 3 -COCl) in the presence of anhydrous aluminium chloride (AlCl 3 ) to form alkyl or acetyl substituted benzene or its derivative, this reaction is called Friedel-Crafts reaction. Friedel-Crafts alkylation: When any benzene or its derivative is treated with alkyl halides (R-X, X=Cl, Br) in the presence of anhydrous aluminium chloride (AlCl 3 ) to form alkyl substituted benzene or its derivatives, this reaction is called Friedel-Crafts alkylation. For example: Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Explanation: Aniline is a Lewis base (electron-pair acceptor) while AlCl 3 is Lewis acid (electron-pair donor). They combine with each to form a salt. Due to the presence of a positive charge on nitrogen (N) atom in the salt, the group N + H 2 AlCl 3 - acts as a strong electron-withdrawing group (strong deactivating group). As a result, it reduces the electron density in the benzene ring and hence aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts (alkylation or acetylation) reaction Diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines. Explanation: Aromatic amine form arene diazonium salts, which are stable for a short time in solution at low temperature(273-278K).The diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable due to the dispersal of the positive charge on the benzene ring (resonance) as shown below: The aliphatic amines, on the other hand, form highly unstable alkane diazonium salt (R—N 2 + Cl - ). They rapidly decompose even at low temperatures (<272-278K) forming carbocation and nitrogen gas. Hence, diazonium salts of aromatic amine are much more stable than aliphatic diazonium salts. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is a very convenient method for the preparation of pure aliphatic amines ( especially primary amines) Phthalimide on treatment with ethanolic KOH gives potassium phthalimide which on heating with a suitable alkyl hallide gives N-substituted phthalimides. These upon subsequent hydrolysis with dil.HCl under pressure or with alkali gives primary amines. Step 1: Phthalimide is treated with KOH to form potassium phthalimide Step 2: Potassium phthalimide is treated with suitable alkyl halide to form N-substituted phthalimides. Step 3: N-substituted phthalimides undergoes hydrolysis in the prsence of dil HCl or with alkali(NaOH) to give primary amines. Overall reaction: {tex}\therefore{/tex} Gabriel phthalimide synthesis results in the formation of primary(1° amine) only. Secondary or tertiary amines are not formed through this synthesis. Hence, Gabriel phthalimide synthesis preferred for the formation of primary amines only.
solution_html:
1. Pkb value is the negative logarithm of the basicity constant (Kb) .i.e., pKb = -logKb
    Evidently, smaller the value of pKb, stronger is the base (strong tendency to donate electrons)
    = The structure of methylamine is CH3NH2
    = The structure of aniline is:

    Aniline (C6H5NH2) shows resonance:

    As a result of resonance, the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom gets delocalized over the benzene ring. As a result, electron density on the nitrogen decreases and thus is less easily available to donate electrons making it less basic. In contrast, in methylamine (CH3NH2), the delocalization of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom by resonance is not possible. Furthermore, CH3 being an electron- releasing group, due to which +I effect of CH3 increases the electron density on the N- atom and thus is more easily available to donate electrons making it more basic.

    Therefore, aniline is a weaker base than methylamine and hence its pKb value is higher than that of methylamine.

  2. Ethylamine when added to water form H-bonds with water. It dissolves in water due to intermolecular H-bonding as shown below:

    Thus, ethylamine is soluble in water.
    However, in aniline, due to the larger hydrophobic part, i.e., hydrocarbon part (C6H5 group), which tends to retard the formation of H-bonds. The extent of H-bonding decreases and hence aniline is insoluble in water.

  3. The structure of methylamine is CH3NH2
    Methylamine is more basic than water due to the presence of CH3 (electron releasing group) and +I effect (pushing electrons). Being more basic, methylamine accepts a proton from water liberating OH- ions.

    Dissociation of ferric chloride in water to give Fe3+ and Cl-
    FeCl3 → Fe3+ + 3Cl-
    Now, the liberated OH- ions combine with Fe3+ ions present in H2O to form a brown precipitate of hydrated ferric oxide.

  4. The below diagram shows the position of ortho(o), para(p) and meta(m) derivatives of the amino group:

    Electrophilic addition reaction of amines: In addition to the reaction of the amino group (NH2 group), aromatic amines also undergo typical electrophilic substitution reactions of the aromatic ring. In all these reactions, the NH2 group strongly activates the aromatic ring through delocalization of the lone pair of electrons of the N-atom over the aromatic ring.

    As a result, electron density increases more at ortho and para positions. Therefore NH2 group directs the incoming group to ortho and para positions, i.e., NH2 is an o-, p-directing group.
    But it has been observed that on nitration of aniline gives a substantial amount of m-nitro aniline.
    Explanation: Nitration is usually carried out in acidic medium in the presence of concentrated HNO3 and concentrated H2SO4. As a result, most of the aniline is converted into anilinium ion(gets protonated) and since - +NH3 is an m-directing group, therefore, an unexpectedly large amount of m-nitroaniline is obtained.
    Nitration of aniline gives mainly p-nitroaniline

    Nitration of anilinium ions give m-nitro aniline(due to protonation)

    Hence, the nitration of aniline gives a mixture of p-nitroaniline and m-nitroaniline in approx. 1:1 ratio.

  5. Friedel- Crafts reaction: When any benzene or its derivative is treated with an alkyl halide (R-X, X=Cl) or acetyl chloride (CH3-COCl) in the presence of anhydrous aluminium chloride (AlCl3) to form alkyl or acetyl substituted benzene or its derivative, this reaction is called Friedel-Crafts reaction.
    Friedel-Crafts alkylation: When any benzene or its derivative is treated with alkyl halides (R-X, X=Cl, Br) in the presence of anhydrous aluminium chloride (AlCl3) to form alkyl substituted benzene or its derivatives, this reaction is called Friedel-Crafts alkylation. For example:

    Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
    Explanation: Aniline is a Lewis base (electron-pair acceptor) while AlCl3 is Lewis acid (electron-pair donor). They combine with each to form a salt.

    Due to the presence of a positive charge on nitrogen (N) atom in the salt, the group N+H2AlCl3- acts as a strong electron-withdrawing group (strong deactivating group). As a result, it reduces the electron density in the benzene ring and hence aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts (alkylation or acetylation) reaction

  6. Diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines.
    Explanation: Aromatic amine form arene diazonium salts, which are stable for a short time in solution at low temperature(273-278K).The diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable due to the dispersal of the positive charge on the benzene ring (resonance) as shown below:

    The aliphatic amines, on the other hand, form highly unstable alkane diazonium salt (R—N2+Cl-). They rapidly decompose even at low temperatures (<272-278K) forming carbocation and nitrogen gas.

    Hence, diazonium salts of aromatic amine are much more stable than aliphatic diazonium salts.

  7. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is a very convenient method for the preparation of pure aliphatic amines ( especially primary amines) Phthalimide on treatment with ethanolic KOH gives potassium phthalimide which on heating with a suitable alkyl hallide gives N-substituted phthalimides. These upon subsequent hydrolysis with dil.HCl under pressure or with alkali gives primary amines.
    Step 1: Phthalimide is treated with KOH to form potassium phthalimide

    Step 2: Potassium phthalimide is treated with suitable alkyl halide to form N-substituted phthalimides.

    Step 3: N-substituted phthalimides undergoes hydrolysis in the prsence of dil HCl or with alkali(NaOH) to give primary amines.

    Overall reaction:

    {tex}\therefore{/tex}Gabriel phthalimide synthesis results in the formation of primary(1° amine) only. Secondary or tertiary amines are not formed through this synthesis. Hence, Gabriel phthalimide synthesis preferred for the formation of primary amines only.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Arrange the following: In decreasing order of the pK b values: C 2 H 5 NH 2 , C 6 H 5 NHCH 3 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH and C 6 H 5 NH 2 In increasing order of basic strength: C 6 H 5 NH 2 , C 6 H 5 N(CH 3 ) 2 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH and CH 3 NH 2 In increasing order of basic strength: Aniline, p-nitroaniline and p-toluidine C 6 H 5 NH 2 , C 6 H 5 NHCH 3 , C 6 H 5 CH 2 NH 2 . In decreasing order of basic strength in gas phase: C 2 H 5 NH 2 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N and NH 3 In increasing order of boiling point: C 2 H 5 OH, (CH 3 ) 2 NH, C 2 H 5 NH 2 In increasing order of solubility in water: C 6 H 5 NH 2 , (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH, C 2 H 5 NH 2 .
question_html:
Arrange the following:

  1. In decreasing order of the pKb values:
    C2H5NH2, C6H5NHCH3, (C2H5)2NH and C6H5NH2
  2. In increasing order of basic strength:
    C6H5NH2, C6H5N(CH3)2, (C2H5)2NH and CH3NH2
  3. In increasing order of basic strength:
    1. Aniline, p-nitroaniline and p-toluidine
    2. C6H5NH2, C6H5NHCH3, C6H5CH2NH2.
  4. In decreasing order of basic strength in gas phase:
    C2H5NH2, (C2H5)2NH, (C2H5)3N and NH3
  5. In increasing order of boiling point:
    C2H5OH, (CH3)2NH, C2H5NH2
  6. In increasing order of solubility in water:
    C6H5NH2, (C2H5)2NH, C2H5NH2.
solution_text: The order of pK b will decrease as: C 6 H 5 NH 2 > C 6 H 5 NHCH 3 > C 2 H 5 NH 2 > (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH The increasing order of basicity is - C 6 H 5 NH 2 > C 6 H 5 N(CH 3 ) 2 < CH 3 NH 2 < (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH The increasing order of basic strength is - p-Nitroaniline < Aniline < p-Toluidine The availability of l.p. on N of p-nitroaniline is drastically reduced by the presence of electron-withdrawing -NO 2 group on it. In contrast, the presence of electron releasing -CH 3 group increases the electron density on the N atom and improves basicity in p-toluidine. (b) C 6 H 5 NH 2 < C 6 H 5 NHCH 3 < C 6 H 5 CH 2 NH 2 Involvement of l.p. of N in resonance causes aniline to have low basicity. In II, the -Me group through its +I effect improves the electron density on N and therefore its basic strength increases. In III, the -NH 2 is farther off from benzene ring and hence l.p. is localized on it and hence the basic strength is highest. In the gas phase, basicity follows the order : (C 2 H 5 ) 3 N > (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH > C 2 H 5 NH 2 > NH 3 In the gas phase, the stabilization by solvation is not present and hence basic strength follows the expected order based on +I effect of alkyl groups. Increasing order of boiling point- (CH 3 ) 2 NH < C 2 H 5 NH 2 < C 2 H 5 OH C 6 H 5 NH 2 < (C 2 H 5 ) 2 NH < C 2 H 5 NH 2 Amines can form hydrogen bonds with water and are therefore soluble in it. However, the solubility decreases if the mass of the hydrocarbon part increases.
solution_html:
1. The order of pKb will decrease as:
    C6H5NH2 > C6H5NHCH3 > C2H5NH2 > (C2H5)2 NH
  2. The increasing order of basicity is - C6H5NH2 > C6H5N(CH3)2 < CH3NH2 < (C2H5)2NH
  3.  The increasing order of basic strength is - p-Nitroaniline < Aniline < p-Toluidine
       
    The availability of l.p. on N of p-nitroaniline is drastically reduced by the presence of electron-withdrawing -NO2 group on it.
    In contrast, the presence of electron releasing -CH3 group increases the electron density on the N atom and improves basicity in p-toluidine.
    (b) C6H5NH2 < C6H5NHCH3 < C6H5CH2NH2 Involvement of l.p. of N in resonance causes aniline to have low basicity. In II, the -Me group through its +I effect improves the electron density on N and therefore its basic strength increases. In III, the -NH2 is farther off from benzene ring and hence l.p. is localized on it and hence the basic strength is highest.

  4. In the gas phase, basicity follows the order : (C2H5)3N > (C2H5)2NH > C2H5NH2 > NH3 In the gas phase, the stabilization by solvation is not present and hence basic strength follows the expected order based on +I effect of alkyl groups.

  5. Increasing order of boiling point- (CH3)2NH < C2H5NH2 < C2H5OH 

  6. C6H5NH2 < (C2H5)2NH < C2H5NH2
    Amines can form hydrogen bonds with water and are therefore soluble in it. However, the solubility decreases if the mass of the hydrocarbon part increases.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Describe a method for the identification of primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Also write chemical equations of the reactions involved.
question_html: Describe a method for the identification of primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Also write chemical equations of the reactions involved.
solution_text: Primary, secondary and tertiary amines can be identified and distinguished by Hinsberg's test. In this test, the amines are allowed to react with Hinsberg's reagent (benzene sulphonyl chloride C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl). Primary amines react with benzenesulphonyl chloride to form N-alkyl benzenesulphonamide which is soluble in alkali. Secondary amines react with Hinsberg's reagent to form N, N-dialkyl benzene sulphonamide which is insoluble in alkali. Tertiary amines do not react with Hinsberg's reagent.
solution_html:
Primary, secondary and tertiary amines can be identified and distinguished by Hinsberg's test. In this test, the amines are allowed to react with Hinsberg's reagent (benzene sulphonyl chloride C6H5SO2Cl).

  1. Primary amines react with benzenesulphonyl chloride to form N-alkyl benzenesulphonamide which is soluble in alkali.
  2. Secondary amines react with Hinsberg's reagent to form N, N-dialkyl benzene sulphonamide which is insoluble in alkali.
  3. Tertiary amines do not react with Hinsberg's reagent.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write short notes on the following: Carbylamine reaction Diazotisation Hofmann’s bromamide reaction Coupling reaction Ammonolysis Acetylation Gabriel phthalimide synthesis
question_html:
Write short notes on the following:

  1. Carbylamine reaction
  2. Diazotisation
  3. Hofmann’s bromamide reaction
  4. Coupling reaction
  5. Ammonolysis
  6. Acetylation
  7. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis
solution_text: Carbylamine reaction: Aliphatic and aromatic primary amines on heating with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide form isocyanides or carbylamines which are foul-smelling substances. Secondary and tertiary amines do not show this reaction. This reaction is known as carbylamine reaction or isocyanide test and is used as a test for primary amines. Diazotisation: The conversion of primary aromatic amines into diazonium salts is known as diazotisation. Due to its instability, the diazonium salt is not generally stored and is used immediately after its preparation. The conversion is brought about by reacting the amine with HNO 2 which is prepared in situ. Hofmann’s bromamide reaction: Primary amides when heated with Br 2 and (aqueous or ethanolic solution of) NaOH lose a carbon atom and are converted to the corresponding amines. It is an example of a step-down reaction. Coupling reaction: The reaction of diazonium salts with phenols and aromatic amines to form azo compounds having an extended conjugated system with both aromatic rings joined through the — N = N — bond, is called coupling reaction. In this reaction, the nitrogen atoms of the diazo group are retained in the product. The coupling with phenols takes place in mildly alkaline medium while that with amines occurs under faintly acidic conditions. For example, Coupling generally occurs at the p-position with respect to the hydroxyl or the amino group, if free, otherwise it takes place at the o-position. Ammonolysis: The process of cleavage of the C — X bond in alkyl halides by ammonia molecule is called ammonolysis. 1° amine thus obtained behaves as a nucleophile and further reacts with an alkyl halide to form 2°, 3°, and finally quaternary ammonium salt. Ammomolysis has the disadvantage of yielding primary, secondary and tertiary amines. Acetylation : The process of introducing an acetyl group {tex}(CH_3-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-){/tex} into a molecule is called acetylation. The reaction occurs by nucleophilic substitution. There occurs a replacement of hydrogen atom of -NH 2 or > NH by the acetyl group. Common acetylating agents used are acetyl chloride and acetic anhydride. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: used for the preparation of primary amines. In this reaction, phthalimide is converted into its potassium salt of phthalimide by treating it with alcoholic potassium hydroxide. Then potassium phthalimide is heated with an alkyl halide to yield an N-alkylpthalimide which is hydrolyzed to phthalic acid and primary amine by alkaline hydrolysis. This synthesis is very useful for the preparation of pure aralkyl and aliphatic primary amines. However, aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by this method because aryl halide do not undergo nucleophilic substitution reaction.
solution_html:
1. Carbylamine reaction: Aliphatic and aromatic primary amines on heating with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide form isocyanides or carbylamines which are foul-smelling substances. Secondary and tertiary amines do not show this reaction. This reaction is known as carbylamine reaction or isocyanide test and is used as a test for primary amines.

  2. Diazotisation: The conversion of primary aromatic amines into diazonium salts is known as diazotisation. Due to its instability, the diazonium salt is not generally stored and is used immediately after its preparation.
    The conversion is brought about by reacting the amine with HNO2 which is prepared in situ.

  3. Hofmann’s bromamide reaction: Primary amides when heated with Br2 and (aqueous or ethanolic solution of) NaOH lose a carbon atom and are converted to the corresponding amines. It is an example of a step-down reaction.

  4. Coupling reaction: The reaction of diazonium salts with phenols and aromatic amines to form azo compounds having an extended conjugated system with both aromatic rings joined through the — N = N — bond, is called coupling reaction. In this reaction, the nitrogen atoms of the diazo group are retained in the product. The coupling with phenols takes place in mildly alkaline medium while that with amines occurs under faintly acidic conditions. For example,

    Coupling generally occurs at the p-position with respect to the hydroxyl or the amino group, if free, otherwise it takes place at the o-position.

  5. Ammonolysis: The process of cleavage of the C — X bond in alkyl halides by ammonia molecule is called ammonolysis. 1° amine thus obtained behaves as a nucleophile and further reacts with an alkyl halide to form 2°, 3°, and finally quaternary ammonium salt. Ammomolysis has the disadvantage of yielding primary, secondary and tertiary amines.

  6. Acetylation: The process of introducing an acetyl group {tex}(CH_3-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-){/tex} into a molecule is called acetylation. The reaction occurs by nucleophilic substitution. There occurs a replacement of hydrogen atom of -NH2 or > NH by the acetyl group.
    Common acetylating agents used are acetyl chloride and acetic anhydride.

  7. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis: used for the preparation of primary amines. In this reaction, phthalimide is converted into its potassium salt of phthalimide by treating it with alcoholic potassium hydroxide. Then potassium phthalimide is heated with an alkyl halide to yield an N-alkylpthalimide which is hydrolyzed to phthalic acid and primary amine by alkaline hydrolysis.

    This synthesis is very useful for the preparation of pure aralkyl and aliphatic primary amines. However, aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by this method because aryl halide do not undergo nucleophilic substitution reaction.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Give the structures of A, B and C in the following reactions: {tex}CH_3CH_2I\xrightarrow{NaCN}A\xrightarrow[{Partial\;hydrolysis}]{OH^-}B\xrightarrow{NaOH+Br_2}C{/tex} {tex}C_6H_5N_2CI\xrightarrow{CuCN}A\xrightarrow{H_2O/H^+}B\xrightarrow[\Delta]{NH_3}C{/tex} {tex}CH_3CH_2Br\xrightarrow{KCN}A\xrightarrow{LiAIH_4}B\xrightarrow[{O^oC}]{HNO_2}C{/tex} {tex}C_6H_5NO_2\xrightarrow{Fe/HCI}A\xrightarrow[{273\;K}]{NaNO_2+HCI}B\xrightarrow[\triangle]{H_2O/H^+}C{/tex} {tex}CH_3COOH\xrightarrow[\triangle]{NH_3}A\xrightarrow{NaOBr}B\xrightarrow{NaNO_2/HCI}C{/tex} {tex}C_6H_2NO_2\xrightarrow{Fe/HCI}A\xrightarrow[{273\;K}]{HNO_2}B\xrightarrow{C_6H_5OH}C{/tex}
question_html:
Give the structures of A, B and C in the following reactions:

  1. {tex}CH_3CH_2I\xrightarrow{NaCN}A\xrightarrow[{Partial\;hydrolysis}]{OH^-}B\xrightarrow{NaOH+Br_2}C{/tex}
  2. {tex}C_6H_5N_2CI\xrightarrow{CuCN}A\xrightarrow{H_2O/H^+}B\xrightarrow[\Delta]{NH_3}C{/tex}
  3. {tex}CH_3CH_2Br\xrightarrow{KCN}A\xrightarrow{LiAIH_4}B\xrightarrow[{O^oC}]{HNO_2}C{/tex}
  4. {tex}C_6H_5NO_2\xrightarrow{Fe/HCI}A\xrightarrow[{273\;K}]{NaNO_2+HCI}B\xrightarrow[\triangle]{H_2O/H^+}C{/tex}
  5. {tex}CH_3COOH\xrightarrow[\triangle]{NH_3}A\xrightarrow{NaOBr}B\xrightarrow{NaNO_2/HCI}C{/tex}
  6. {tex}C_6H_2NO_2\xrightarrow{Fe/HCI}A\xrightarrow[{273\;K}]{HNO_2}B\xrightarrow{C_6H_5OH}C{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: An aromatic compound 'A' on treatment with aqueous ammonia and heating forms compound 'B' which on heating with Br 2 and KOH forms a compound 'C' of molecular formula C 6 H 7 N. Write the structures and IUPAC names of compounds A, B and C.
question_html: An aromatic compound 'A' on treatment with aqueous ammonia and heating forms compound 'B' which on heating with Br2 and KOH forms a compound 'C' of molecular formula C6H7N. Write the structures and IUPAC names of compounds A, B and C.
solution_text: It is given that compound 'C' having the molecular formula, C 6 H 7 N, and formed by the reaction of compound 'B' with Br 2 and KOH. This is a Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction. Therefore, compound 'B' is an amide, and compound 'C' is an amine. The only amine having the molecular formula C 6 H 7 N is aniline, (C 6 H 5 NH 2 ). Compound 'B' (from which 'C' is formed) must be benzamide, ​​​​​​​(C 6 H 5 CONH 2 ). Further, benzamide is formed by heating compound 'A' with aqueous ammonia. Therefore, compound 'A' must be benzoic acid. The given reactions can be explained with the help of the following equations: ​​​​​​​
solution_html:
It is given that compound 'C' having the molecular formula, C6H7N,  and formed by the reaction of compound 'B' with Br2 and KOH. This is a Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction. Therefore, compound 'B' is an amide, and compound 'C' is an amine. The only amine having the molecular formula C6H7N is aniline, (C6H5NH2).

Compound 'B' (from which 'C' is formed) must be benzamide, ​​​​​​​(C6H5CONH2).

Further, benzamide is formed by heating compound 'A' with aqueous ammonia. Therefore, compound 'A' must be benzoic acid.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Why cannot aromatic primary amines be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis?
question_html: Why cannot aromatic primary amines be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis?
solution_text: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis involves nucleophilic substitution of alkyl halides by the anion formed by the phthalimide but aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by the phthalimide hence aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by this method.
solution_html: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis involves nucleophilic substitution of alkyl halides by the anion formed by the phthalimide but aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with the anion formed by the phthalimide hence aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by this method.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Write the reactions of aromatic and aliphatic primary amines with nitrous acid.
question_html:
Write the reactions of

  1. aromatic and
  2. aliphatic primary amines with nitrous acid.
solution_text: Aromatic amines react with nitrous acid (prepared in situ from NaNO 2 and a mineral acid such as HCl) at 273 - 278 K to form stable aromatic diazonium salts, NaCl and H 2 O Aliphatic primary amines react with nitrous acid (prepared in situ from​​​​​​​ NaNO 2 and a mineral acid such as HCl) to form unstable aliphatic diazonium salts, which further produce alcohol and HCl with the evolution of ​​​​​​​N 2 gas. ​​​​​​
solution_html:
1. Aromatic amines react with nitrous acid (prepared in situ from NaNO2 and a mineral acid such as HCl) at 273 - 278 K to form stable aromatic diazonium salts, NaCl and H2O
  2. Aliphatic primary amines react with nitrous acid (prepared in situ from​​​​​​​ NaNO2 and a mineral acid such as HCl) to form unstable aliphatic diazonium salts, which further produce alcohol and HCl with the evolution of ​​​​​​​N2 gas.
    ​​​​​​

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Give plausible explanation for each of the following: Why are amines less acidic than alcohols of comparable molecular masses? Why do primary amines have higher boiling point than tertiary amines? Why are aliphatic amines stronger bases than aromatic amines?
question_html:
Give plausible explanation for each of the following:

  1. Why are amines less acidic than alcohols of comparable molecular masses?
  2. Why do primary amines have higher boiling point than tertiary amines?
  3. Why are aliphatic amines stronger bases than aromatic amines?
solution_text: Loss of a proton from an amine gives RNH – ion while the loss of a proton from alcohol gives RO- ion as shown below : R - NH 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R - NH - + H + R - O - H {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R - O - + H + As O is more electronegative than N, RO – can accommodate the negative charge more easily than the RNH – can. As, RO – is more stable than RNH – the former is formed more. As a result, amines are less acidic than alcohols. A primary amine is engaged in intermolecular association due to hydrogen bonding between the nitrogen of one nad hydrogen of other while tertiary amine does not have intermolecular association due to the absence of hydrogen atom therefore the boiling point of primary amine is more than tertiary amine. The basic nature of amines is a result of the presence of l.p. of electron on the N atom. Also, the electron density is increased on N due to the +I effect of the alkyl group. In aryl amines, the l.p. on N is involved in resonance with the benzene ring and hence less available for protonation. In aliphatic amines, there is no such delocalisation and hence it is more basic.
solution_html:
1. Loss of a proton from an amine gives RNH– ion while the loss of a proton from alcohol gives RO- ion as shown below :
    R - NH2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R - NH- + H+
    R - O - H {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R - O- + H+
    As O is more electronegative than N, RO– can accommodate the negative charge more easily than the RNH– can.
    As, RO– is more stable than RNH– the former is formed more. As a result, amines are less acidic than alcohols.
  2. A primary amine is engaged in intermolecular association due to hydrogen bonding between the nitrogen of one nad hydrogen of other while tertiary amine does not have intermolecular association due to the absence of hydrogen atom therefore the boiling point of primary amine is more than tertiary amine.

    1. The basic nature of amines is a result of the presence of l.p. of electron on the N atom. Also, the electron density is increased on N due to the +I effect of the alkyl group.

    2. In aryl amines, the l.p. on N is involved in resonance with the benzene ring and hence less available for protonation.

    3. In aliphatic amines, there is no such delocalisation and hence it is more basic.
